en.extract:
The Amami woodcock (Scolopax mira) is a medium-sized wader. It is slightly larger and longer-legged than the Eurasian woodcock.
This species is a restricted-range endemic found only in forests on Amami Ōshima, Okinawa and Tokunoshima, including some smaller, nearby islands (Kakeromajima and Tokashiki).
Due to the introduction of the invasive small Indian mongoose, their population has been declining. Efforts to control the mongoose population have led to recovery of Amami woodcock, as documented on Amami where the mongoose has been successfully eradicated. Insofar as its habits are known, they are similar to the Eurasian woodcock.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Amami woodcock was originally described as a subspecies of the Eurasian woodcock, due to a juvenile that resembled the Eurasian woodcock in coloration. Later, some argued that the Amami woodcock was a distinct species—Kobayashi in 1979 and  Cramp & Simmons in 1983. Comparison between the two species revealed their distinct physical features, and led to the emergence of the Amami woodcock as a distinct species.


References


Further reading
Shorebirds by Hayman, Marchant and Prater, ISBN 0-7099-2034-2


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet.

| status_ref = <ref name=IUCN>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International. |year=2025 |title=''Scolopax mira'' |volume=2025 |article-number=e.T22693056A252804793 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2025-2.RLTS.T22693056A252804793.en |access-date=3 December 2025}}</ref>
| taxon = Scolopax mira
| authority = [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1916
}}
The '''Amami woodcock''' ('''''Scolopax mira''''') is a medium-sized [[wader]]. It is slightly larger and longer-legged than the [[Eurasian woodcock]].

This species is a [[restricted-range endemic]] found only in forests on [[Amami Ōshima]], [[Okinawa Island|Okinawa]] and [[Tokunoshima]],<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/amawoo1/cur/introduction | title=Amami Woodcock (Scolopax mira), version 1.0 | journal=Birds of the World | date=2020 | last1=Van Gils | first1=Jan | last2=Wiersma | first2=Popko | last3=Kirwan | first3=Guy M. }}</ref> including some smaller, nearby islands ([[Kakeromajima]] and [[Tokashiki, Okinawa|Tokashiki]]).<ref name=IUCN/>
Due to the introduction of the invasive [[small Indian mongoose]], their population has been declining.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1007/s10336-015-1250-1 | title=Endemic Amami Jay, invasive Small Indian Mongoose, and other alien organisms: A new century investigation of island aliens towards improved ecosystem management | date=2015 | last1=Ishida | first1=K. | last2=Murata | first2=K. | last3=Nishiumi | first3=I. | last4=Takahashi | first4=Y. | last5=Takashi | first5=M. | journal=Journal of Ornithology | volume=156 | pages=209–216 | doi-access=free }}</ref> Efforts to control the mongoose population have led to recovery of Amami woodcock, as documented on Amami where the mongoose has been successfully eradicated.<ref name=IUCN/> Insofar as its habits are known, they are similar to the Eurasian woodcock.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The Amami woodcock was originally described as a subspecies of the [[Eurasian woodcock]], due to a juvenile that resembled the Eurasian woodcock in coloration. Later, some argued that the Amami woodcock was a distinct species—Kobayashi in 1979 and  [[Stanley Cramp|Cramp]] & Simmons in 1983. Comparison between the two species revealed their distinct physical features, and led to the emergence of the Amami woodcock as a distinct species.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Brazil|first1=Mark|last2=Ikenaga|first2=Hiroshi|date=December 1987|title=The Amami Woodcock Scolopax mira: Its Identity and Identification|url=http://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/07/Brazil-AmamiWoodcock.pdf|journal=Forktail}}</ref>
